title: GitLab CE/EE WebAuthn 2FA Bypass CVE-2026-2745 (CVSS 6.8): Inconsistent Input Validation Allows Unauthenticated Account Takeover — Affecting All Versions from 7.11 to 18.10.0, Patch Released March 25

sources.name: GitLab Patch Release: 18.10.1, 18.9.3, 18.8.7 - Official Release Notes
BleepingComputer - GitLab warns of high-severity 2FA bypass, denial-of-service flaws
CyberSecurityNews - Multiple GitLab Vulnerabilities Enables 2FA Bypass and DoS Attacks
CSO Online - GitLab 2FA login protection bypass lets attackers take over accounts
InfoWorld - GitLab 2FA login protection bypass lets attackers take over accounts

GBHackers - GitLab Patches Multiple Flaws Allowing Authentication Bypass
Cyberpress - GitLab Patches Multiple Vulnerabilities Including 2FA Bypass

timeline.event: CVE-2026-2745 reserved; vulnerability reported to GitLab via HackerOne bug bounty program by researcher a0xnirudh
GitLab releases patch versions 18.10.1, 18.9.3, and 18.8.7; vulnerability publicly disclosed with CVE details
CVE record published on CVE.org; BleepingComputer and multiple security outlets report the vulnerability
Government CERTs including HKCERT issue security bulletins urging immediate upgrade; CVE information updated
Not yet in CISA KEV; no confirmed active exploitation in the wild reported; high exploitation likelihood given exposure

simple_summary.what_happened: GitLab, the DevOps platform hosting millions of software repositories and CI/CD pipelines, disclosed a serious security flaw in how its two-factor authentication (2FA) works. Normally, logging in requires both a password and a hardware security key (WebAuthn). This vulnerability allows an attacker to skip the hardware key step entirely — meaning if they have or can guess a user's password, they can log straight in without needing the physical device. The flaw affects every version of GitLab going back to 2013 (version 7.11). Patches were released on March 25, 2026.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: All organizations running self-managed GitLab Community Edition (CE) or Enterprise Edition (EE) versions 7.11 through 18.10.0 that have not yet updated to the patched versions (18.8.7, 18.9.3, or 18.10.1). GitLab.com (SaaS) was patched automatically. Particularly at risk are organizations using GitLab to host sensitive source code, secrets, and CI/CD pipelines.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker obtains a GitLab username and password through phishing, credential stuffing, or a data breach. Step 2: Attacker initiates login for the target account, which normally prompts for a WebAuthn hardware key as a second factor. Step 3: Instead of using a legitimate hardware key, the attacker submits a forged or malformed WebAuthn device response that exploits the inconsistent input validation. Step 4: GitLab's authentication incorrectly accepts the forged response and completes the login, bypassing 2FA entirely. Step 5: Attacker now has full access to the victim's account — all repositories, projects, CI/CD pipelines, and stored secrets — and can steal code, inject malicious code, or pivot to production infrastructure.
what_you_should_do: Upgrade all self-managed GitLab instances to version 18.8.7, 18.9.3, or 18.10.1 immediately
If immediate upgrade is not possible, force password resets for all accounts using WebAuthn 2FA as a precautionary measure
Review authentication logs for any suspicious login activity from unexpected geographic locations or devices between February 19 and March 25, 2026
Audit all repositories and CI/CD pipelines for unauthorized changes or new access tokens that may have been added by attackers
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: GitLab repositories often contain source code, API keys, database credentials, infrastructure configuration, and CI/CD pipelines that deploy directly to production environments. A successful account takeover doesn't just expose data — it gives attackers a direct path to inject malicious code into software supply chains. Organizations with thousands of developers may have hundreds of accounts using WebAuthn 2FA, all potentially vulnerable. The fact that this affects all GitLab versions back to 2013 means the exposure window for unpatched deployments is extremely large.

tactics.description: Attacker bypasses WebAuthn 2FA to gain authenticated access to GitLab using valid credentials plus the 2FA bypass, obtaining a fully authenticated session
The vulnerability exploits a flaw in GitLab's authentication process — specifically inconsistent validation of WebAuthn device responses — to bypass multi-factor authentication
Following account takeover, attackers access GitLab repositories containing source code, secrets, API keys, and CI/CD configurations

tactics.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
tactics.description: Attacker injects malicious code into repositories or modifies CI/CD pipelines to distribute backdoored software to downstream users or production environments

detection_guidance.summary: Detection focuses on authentication anomalies in GitLab logs — specifically successful logins that show unusual geographic/device patterns, or authentication flow anomalies where the 2FA challenge was bypassed. Upgrade is the primary mitigation; detection helps identify pre-patch exploitation.

log_sources.description: Monitor for successful authentication events where the 2FA WebAuthn challenge response is abnormal, or where authentication completed without expected device fingerprint
Review admin audit logs for account logins from unexpected IP addresses or countries, new SSH key additions, new access tokens, or repository access from unusual sources post-login
Monitor for unauthorized pipeline modifications, new environment variables added (potential secret injection), or unexpected runner assignments

what_to_look_for.indicator: Successful logins from unexpected geolocations for 2FA-enabled accounts
what_to_look_for.description: Authentication events for accounts using WebAuthn 2FA that originate from IPs or countries not previously associated with that user — especially between Feb 19 and Mar 25, 2026
what_to_look_for.log_field: event='Successful Sign-in' AND two_factor='WebAuthn' AND ip NOT IN known_user_ips

what_to_look_for.indicator: New access tokens or SSH keys added post-login
what_to_look_for.description: After a bypass-assisted login, attackers typically add persistent access tokens or SSH keys to maintain access without needing passwords

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unusual repository access patterns after authentication
what_to_look_for.description: Bulk repository cloning, access to multiple unrelated projects, or large data downloads following authentication may indicate post-exploitation data exfiltration
what_to_look_for.log_field: event='Repository Download' AND size > threshold AND user IN 2fa_users

immediate_actions.action: Upgrade GitLab to patched versions immediately
immediate_actions.description: Upgrade all self-managed GitLab CE/EE instances to version 18.8.7, 18.9.3, or 18.10.1. This is the only complete fix. GitLab.com (SaaS) has already been patched automatically.
immediate_actions.command:
# For Omnibus GitLab installations:
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install gitlab-ee=18.10.1-ee.0
# or
sudo yum update gitlab-ee-18.10.1

immediate_actions.action: Audit authentication logs for suspicious logins since February 19, 2026
immediate_actions.description: Review GitLab authentication logs for all accounts using WebAuthn 2FA for any logins from unexpected locations or devices between February 19 (vulnerability reservation date) and March 25 (patch date). This is the potential exploitation window.
For accounts with owner or maintainer access to sensitive projects, force immediate password reset and re-enrollment of 2FA devices. Revoke any access tokens created between February 19 and March 25.

immediate_actions.action: Review CI/CD pipelines and repository changes for unauthorized modifications
immediate_actions.description: Audit all CI/CD pipeline definitions and recent commits in sensitive repositories for unauthorized changes, particularly any modifications that add external scripts, change deployment targets, or introduce new dependencies.
